title: Tabs & Stepper
intro: Tabs and Stepper look alike — a row of labels with one highlighted — but they answer opposite questions. `wf-tabs` switches between peer views the user can visit in any order; `wf-stepper` tracks ordered, gated progress through a flow with one correct path. Picking the wrong one quietly lies to the user about whether the order matters.

blocks.body:
Reach for `wf-tabs` when you're switching between *peer views of the same thing* — "Overview / Details / History" on a contract record. Every tab is equally valid and the user can move between them freely, in any order. No tab is a prerequisite for another.

Reach for `wf-stepper` when the user is moving through an *ordered, gated progression* — "Select Type → Add Details → Review → Complete." There is a single correct path, steps generally can't be skipped, and each step carries a completion state (pending, active, completed, error). Stepper is the navigation half of the `FormWizard` page pattern.

The test is order. If the sequence is meaningful and gated, it's a Stepper. If the user should be able to jump straight to the third item without touching the first two, it's Tabs. When you're tempted to use Tabs for an ordered flow, you want a Stepper; when you're tempted to use a Stepper for free navigation, you want Tabs.

blocks.title: Line Tabs (default)
blocks.caption: The default `wf-tabs wf-tabs-line` style: peer views with a 2px primary underline marking the active tab. Muted resting labels, a darker hover, and a disabled tab that drops to text-disabled.

blocks.title: Card Tabs
blocks.caption: `wf-tabs-card` swaps the underline for a sunken tab strip where the active tab reads as a raised white card with a small shadow, joined to its panel.

blocks.title: Tabs variants
blocks.intro: Style modifiers go on the `wf-tabs` wrapper; the active state is `wf-tab-active` on the button, and `disabled` is the native HTML attribute. The wrapper holds a `wf-tabs-list` of `wf-tab` buttons plus a `wf-tabs-content` panel.

rows.name: wf-tabs-line
rows.when: Default. Peer views with a 2px underline on the active tab — the everyday choice for record sub-views.
rows.code: The Overview panel.

rows.name: wf-tabs-card
rows.when: The active tab reads as a raised card on a sunken strip. Use when tabs sit directly atop the content they control, like a date-range switcher.
rows.code: ...

rows.name: wf-tabs-pill
rows.when: Rounded pills with a solid primary fill on the active tab. Best for compact in-content filters — "All / Active / Archived."
rows.code: ...

rows.name: wf-tabs-vertical
rows.when: Lays the tab list down the left with a right-edge active marker. Use for settings-style screens with many peer sections.
rows.code: ...

rows.name: wf-badge in a tab
rows.when: Count indicator on a tab label. Drop a `wf-badge wf-badge-sm` (optionally `-secondary` / `-success`) inside the button after the label.

blocks.title: Horizontal Stepper
blocks.caption: `wf-stepper` showing sequential progress: a completed step (filled primary circle with a check), the active step (white circle, primary ring and number), and pending steps (muted outlined circles). Connectors turn primary once the step before them is complete.

blocks.title: Stepper with error state
blocks.caption: `wf-step-error` renders a failed step as a filled danger circle with a "!" indicator and a danger label, sitting between a completed step and the remaining pending steps.

blocks.title: Stepper step states
blocks.intro: State classes go on each `wf-step`. A step holds a `wf-step-indicator` (number, check, or "!") and a `wf-step-label`, with `wf-step-connector` divs between steps. Mark the connector after a finished step `wf-step-connector-completed` so the progress line fills in.

rows.name: wf-step (default)
rows.when: A pending step the user hasn't reached. Muted outlined circle with the step number.

rows.name: wf-step-active
rows.when: The step the user is on. White fill, primary border and number, primary label — the Stepper's equivalent of `wf-tab-active`.

rows.name: wf-step-completed
rows.when: A finished step. Solid primary circle with a check (✓). Pair with `wf-step-connector-completed` on the connector that follows it.

rows.name: wf-step-error
rows.when: A failed step that needs attention — "Payment Failed." Solid danger circle with a "!" and a danger label.

rows.name: wf-step-connector-completed
rows.when: The connector between two steps after the earlier one is done. Turns the line from border-gray to primary so progress reads at a glance.

blocks.title: Stepper layout modifiers
blocks.intro: Layout modifiers go on the `wf-stepper` wrapper.

rows.name: wf-stepper-vertical
rows.when: Stacks steps top-to-bottom with vertical connectors. Wrap label + description in a `wf-step-content` div. Good for order-tracking timelines.
rows.code:
✓
Order Placed Your order has been received
...

rows.name: wf-stepper-compact
rows.when: Shrinks indicators to 24px and labels to 11px for tight headers and breadcrumb-height progress bars.

rows.name: wf-step-description
rows.when: Optional sub-label under a step's title — "Add payment method." Add as a third span (or inside `wf-step-content` when vertical).
rows.code: 3 Payment Add payment method

blocks.title: Tokens these components reach for
blocks.intro: Both components draw their active/complete signal from the same primary token, and the Stepper error from danger — semantic intent, never decoration (see DESIGN §3.1). All values below are the fallbacks declared inline in `navigation.css`.

rows.use: Active tab underline/fill, completed and active step rings, the filled progress connector — the "you are here / done" signal across both components.
`wf-step-error` indicator fill and label. The only non-primary status color either component uses.
Resting tab labels and pending step labels/indicators.
Disabled tab text and step descriptions.
Tabs-list bottom border, pending step-indicator border, and the unfilled connector line.
Sunken strip behind Card tabs and the pending step-indicator fill.
Pill-tab corners and the round step indicators.

blocks.title: Tabs — peer views
blocks.note: The active button carries `wf-tab-active`; switching tabs swaps which button has it and which panel renders in `wf-tabs-content`.

blocks.title: Stepper — gated progression
blocks.code:
✓ Select Type
2 Add Details
3 Review
blocks.note: Completed steps show ✓ and a filled connector; the active step shows its number in a primary ring; pending steps stay muted. This is the header of the FormWizard page pattern.

blocks.title: Tabs vs. Stepper — the navigation choice (DESIGN §4.4)
blocks.intro: From the component decision tree. Peer views? Tabs. Ordered path? Stepper.

items.rule: Use Tabs only for peer views of the same thing, freely navigable in any order.
items.why: Tabs signal that every view is equally valid and order doesn't matter — that's the contract users read from the shape.

items.rule: Use Stepper only for ordered, gated progression with a single correct path and per-step completion state.
items.why: A Stepper promises sequence and gating. Steps generally can't be skipped, and each one tracks pending / active / completed / error.

items.rule: If you're tempted to use Tabs for an ordered flow, you want a Stepper.
items.why: Tabs let users jump ahead and skip prerequisites — exactly what a gated flow must prevent.

items.rule: If you're tempted to use a Stepper for free navigation, you want Tabs.
items.why: A Stepper's completion states and gating imply rules that don't exist for peer views, confusing the user about what's required.

items.rule: Don't reach for Tabs or Stepper for top-level location — that's Sidebar — or for hierarchy crumbs — that's Breadcrumb.
items.why: Tabs and Stepper are in-page navigation; Sidebar answers "where am I in the app" and Breadcrumb answers "where am I in the tree."

items.do: Tabs for "Overview / History / Settings" on one record
items.why: An ordered, gated flow is a Stepper (DESIGN §4.4 / §7.5). Tabs would let users skip straight to Submit.

items.do: Stepper for "Select Type → Add Details → Review → Complete"
items.why: Completion states and gating imply a required sequence that doesn't exist for peer views — use `wf-tabs-vertical` instead.

items.do: `<button class="wf-tab wf-tab-active">` for the current tab
items.dont: Styling the active tab with primary color by hand
items.why: `wf-tab-active` already applies `--wf-color-primary` to text and the underline; hand-rolling it drifts from the token contract (DESIGN §2.7).

items.dont: Leaving every connector gray while steps complete
items.why: The filled connector is the at-a-glance progress signal; without it the Stepper looks stalled even as steps complete.

items.do: Reserve `wf-step-error` for an actual failed step
items.dont: Using the danger color to make a step "stand out"
items.why: Status color is reserved for status (DESIGN §3.1) — a decorative red step reads as a failure that isn't there.

items.title: Tabs and Stepper are static markup, not stateful widgets
items.detail: Nothing in `navigation.css` wires click-to-switch. `wf-tab-active`, `wf-step-active`, and `wf-step-completed` are presentational classes you move by hand (or via prototype JS). The library renders the state; it doesn't manage it.

items.title: The completed connector is a separate element you must flip
items.detail: `wf-step-connector` defaults to the gray border color. It only turns primary when you also add `wf-step-connector-completed` — and that class lives on the connector div, not on the step before it. Forget it and progress won't read.

items.title: Vertical steppers need a wrapping wf-step-content
items.detail: In `wf-stepper-vertical`, the label and description sit in a `wf-step-content` div beside the indicator (the horizontal layout stacks them as bare sibling spans). Swapping layout means restructuring the step's children, not just the wrapper class.

items.title: The step indicator's glyph is content, not derived
items.detail: The ✓ for completed, the number for active/pending, and the "!" for error are literal text inside `wf-step-indicator` — the state class only colors the circle. Set the matching glyph yourself when you change a step's state.

items.title: Card and pill tabs drop the underline entirely
items.detail: `wf-tabs-line` is the only variant that uses the bottom border; `wf-tabs-card`, `wf-tabs-pill`, and `wf-tabs-vertical` each zero out `border-bottom` and signal the active tab a different way (raised card, solid pill, right-edge marker). Don't expect an underline outside the line variant.
